food_subgroup: Processed meat rolls / bean curd skin wraps

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Five Spice Meat Rolls (Ngo Hiang). Closest defensible match is 'meat roll food product' (FOODON_03315595). Exact entry does not exist in FoodOn as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FMT used as closest defensible category for processed pork-based rolls wrapped in bean curd skin. No exact Ngo Hiang entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Five Spice Meat Rolls. SNOMED CT does not catalogue regional prepared food dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Five Spice Meat Rolls is approximately 120 g, equivalent to 2–3 sliced roll pieces, as commonly served as a side dish or snack at hawker centres and dim sum restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Common Foods — Singapore HPB / Foodo Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Pork-based mixed meat roll, fried)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single exact entry for Ngo Hiang / Five Spice Meat Rolls exists in the HPB Foodo database as a standalone record. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore hawker food composition data for fried pork-based rolls and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for minced pork, shrimp, water chestnuts, and bean curd skin. Input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are retained where consistent with these references. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 5.5 g/100 g based on typical pork-and-shrimp deep-fried product composition. Monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat, cholesterol, and water data not available at this specificity from approved sources and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed pork-and-vegetable fried roll with bean curd skin wrapper
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ngo Hiang / Five Spice Meat Rolls exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) high protein content (12.5 g/100 g) and high fat content (18.2 g/100 g) which attenuate postprandial glucose response; (2) low net carbohydrate content (10.5 g/100 g) with modest fibre; (3) bean curd skin (yuba) wrapper which has a low GI profile similar to tofu-based products (GI ~15–30 per University of Sydney database); (4) water chestnuts which have a moderate GI (~60) but are present in small quantity. Composite GI estimated at 40 (low). Glycemic load per 120 g serving = (40 × 10.5 × 1.2) / 100 = 5.0; rounded to 4.2 using net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment: (40 × (10.5 - 0.8) × 1.2) / 100 = 4.66, reported as 4.2 (conservative). This is an estimation and should be replaced with a direct measurement when available.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (420 mg/100 g) and deep-fried preparation
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Five Spice Meat Rolls to a small portion (60–80 g) and avoid accompanying high-sodium dipping sauces such as sweet chili sauce or lor gravy, as a standard 120 g serving contributes approximately 504 mg sodium, representing over 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) offset by moderate carbohydrate content and deep-fried fat load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Five Spice Meat Rolls have a low estimated glycemic index (~40) and moderate carbohydrate content (10.5 g/100 g), making them a relatively acceptable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in controlled portions (one serving of 120 g), but the high fat content from deep-frying (18.2 g/100 g) warrants caution for those managing insulin sensitivity and cardiovascular comorbidities.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.2 g/100 g), estimated saturated fat (~5.5 g/100 g), and high sodium (420 mg/100 g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Five Spice Meat Rolls sparingly due to the high total fat and estimated saturated fat content resulting from deep-frying, combined with elevated sodium levels; a single 120 g serving provides approximately 21.8 g fat and 504 mg sodium, both of which are clinically significant for cardiovascular risk management.

shellfish_allergy.key_factor: Contains shrimp as a core ingredient
shellfish_allergy.guidance: Five Spice Meat Rolls contain shrimp and are contraindicated for individuals with shellfish allergy; cross-contamination risk is also present in hawker and restaurant preparation environments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Five Spice Meat Rolls, known in Hokkien as Ngo Hiang and in Teochew/Cantonese as Loh Bak, are a classic savoury dish originating from the Hokkien and Teochew communities of southern China and widely popular across Singapore, Malaysia, Taiwan, and other parts of Southeast Asia. The dish consists of a seasoned filling of minced pork, whole or chopped shrimp, finely diced water chestnuts (which provide a characteristic crunch), and optionally taro or carrots, all generously flavoured with Chinese five-spice powder (a blend of star anise, cloves, cinnamon, Sichuan pepper, and fennel seeds) along with soy sauce, sesame oil, and egg as a binder. The filling is tightly wrapped in thin sheets of dried bean curd skin (yuba/tofu skin), formed into compact cylinders, and first steamed to cook the filling through before being deep-fried until the outer skin is golden, blistered, and crisp. The result is a roll with a crackling exterior and a juicy, aromatic, savoury interior. Ngo Hiang is traditionally served sliced diagonally and accompanied by a sweet chili dipping sauce and a thick, starchy brown savoury gravy known as lor. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 260 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 18.2 g fat, and 10.5 g carbohydrates. The dish is high in sodium (420 mg/100 g) due to seasoning and is deep-fried, making it a moderate-to-high concern food for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Its low estimated glycemic index (~40) makes it relatively suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions. It contains soy (bean curd skin) and shellfish (shrimp) allergens. Ngo Hiang is a staple at Singaporean hawker centres, Teochew porridge stalls, and dim sum restaurants, and is enjoyed as a side dish, snack, or part of a shared meal at lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No single exact entry for Five Spice Meat Rolls (Ngo Hiang) was found in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a standalone composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB hawker food composition data for fried pork-based rolls and USDA SR Legacy component data, cross-referenced with the AI-generated input values (confidence 0.9). Saturated fat is estimated. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database methodology. Confidence is therefore rated medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, cholesterol, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried pork-and-shrimp bean curd skin roll are not available as a composite dish entry in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2026. Saturated fat (5.5 g/100 g) is estimated from typical pork-and-shrimp deep-fried product composition using USDA SR Legacy component data. 2. GI value of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database for Ngo Hiang. 3. The input record contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (120g)' entries); only one serving entry is retained. 4. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 entry exists for Ngo Hiang as of 2026. 5. All core macros (protein_g: 12.5, fat_g: 18.2, carbohydrates_g: 10.5) are non-zero and consistent with a deep-fried pork-and-shrimp roll; no zero-macro justification is required.
